title: Ensure server parameter 'audit_log_events' has  'CONNECTION' set for MySQL Database Server

input_row.title: Ensure server parameter 'audit_log_events' has  'CONNECTION' set for MySQL Database Server
input_row.description: Set audit_log_enabled to include CONNECTION on MySQL Servers.
input_row.rationale: Enabling CONNECTION helps MySQL Database to log items such as successful and failed connection attempts to the server. Log data can be used to identify, troubleshoot, and repair configuration errors and suboptimal performance.
input_row.audit: From Azure Portal 1. From Azure Home select the Portal Menu. 2. Select Azure Database for MySQL servers. 3. Select a database. 4. Under Settings, select Server parameters. 5. Ensure audit_log_enabled parameter is set to ON. 6. Ensure audit_log_events parameter has CONNECTION checked.
input_row.remediation: From Azure Portal 1. From Azure Home select the Portal Menu. 2. Select Azure Database for MySQL servers. 3. Select a database. 4. Under Settings, select Server parameters. 5. Update audit_log_enabled parameter to ON. 6. Update audit_log_events parameter to have at least CONNECTION checked. 7. Click Save. 8. Under Monitoring, select Diagnostic settings. 9. Select + Add diagnostic setting. 10. Provide a diagnostic setting name. 11. Under Categories, select MySQL Audit Logs. 12. Specify destination details. 13. Click Save. It may take up to 10 minutes for the logs to appear in the configured destination.
input_row.impact: There are further costs incurred for storage of logs. For high traffic databases these logs will be significant. Determine your organization's needs before enabling.
input_row.additional_information: There is also a CLI version: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/mysql/single- server/how-to-configure-audit-logs-cli

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. From the AWS Management Console, navigate to the RDS service.
2. Click on "Databases" in the left navigation pane to see your list of RDS instances.
3. Select the RDS MySQL instance you want to audit.
4. Under the "Configuration" tab, scroll down to the "DB Parameter Group" section and click on the linked parameter group name.
5. In the "Parameters" section, look for the parameter named `audit_log_enabled`.
6. Ensure that the `audit_log_enabled` parameter is set to "ON".
7. Additionally, check for the parameter `audit_log_events` and confirm that it includes "CONNECTION".
8. Confirm that both parameters are set correctly to enforce appropriate logging of connection attempts.

**What to verify/validate:** 
- Ensure `audit_log_enabled` is ON.
- Ensure `audit_log_events` contains "CONNECTION".

**Expected secure configuration:**
- `audit_log_enabled`: ON
- `audit_log_events`: should include "CONNECTION".

**How to confirm compliance:**
- If both parameters are set as expected, the control is compliant. Document the settings for audit purposes. If changes are necessary, follow the RDS documentation for updating DB parameter groups.
